topic.title: N-ary Trees
topic.description: Non-binary tree structures where each node can have up to N children, with preorder, postorder, and level-order traversals all running in O(n).

lessons.title: N-ary Tree Basics
lessons.conceptMd:
## N-ary Tree Basics

A **non-binary tree** (also called an **N-ary tree**) is a rooted tree in which each node can have zero or more child nodes, up to a maximum of N children. When N = 2 the structure is the familiar binary tree; when N > 2 it is called a non-binary or N-ary tree.

### Node Structure

A common implementation uses a **left-child / right-sibling** representation. Rather than storing an array of up to N children (which wastes space when nodes have far fewer children), each node holds:

- `data` — the stored value
- `parent` — pointer to the parent node
- `leftmostChild` — pointer to the first (leftmost) child
- `rightSibling` — pointer to the next sibling

This transforms any N-ary tree into an equivalent binary tree: following `leftmostChild` moves down the first child, and following `rightSibling` moves across siblings.

An alternative doubly-linked variant also stores `leftSibling` and `rightmostChild` for O(1) access in both sibling directions.

### Applications

N-ary trees model hierarchical data naturally:

- **Document structure**: a book node has chapter children, chapters have section children, sections have subsection children.
- **Organizational hierarchy**: a university has college children, colleges have department children.
- **Filesystem**: a directory node has file and subdirectory children.
- **Family trees**: a person node has children as child nodes.

### Relationship to Binary Trees

Every N-ary tree can be converted to a binary tree using the left-child / right-sibling encoding. This means binary tree algorithms can be reused directly, which is why this representation is common in practice.
lessons.pseudocodeMd:
```pseudocode
class Node:
  data
  parent
  leftmostChild    // first child (or null if leaf)
  rightSibling     // next sibling (or null if last)

class Tree:
  root

// Create a new leaf node
NEW-NODE(value):
  node = new Node()
  node.data = value
  node.parent = null
  node.leftmostChild = null
  node.rightSibling  = null
  return node

// Add child as rightmost child of parent
ADD-CHILD(parent, child):
  child.parent = parent
  if parent.leftmostChild == null:
    parent.leftmostChild = child
  else:
    sibling = parent.leftmostChild
    while sibling.rightSibling != null:
      sibling = sibling.rightSibling
    sibling.rightSibling = child
```

lessons.title: Tree Traversals
lessons.conceptMd:
## Tree Traversals

N-ary trees support three primary traversal orders. There is no natural definition of **inorder** for N-ary trees (inorder requires knowing which child is the "middle" child), so only preorder, postorder, and level-order are defined.

### Preorder (CLR — Current, Left children, Right sibling)

Visit the current node **before** its children. Using the left-child / right-sibling representation:
1. If `p == null`, return.
2. Visit `p`.
3. Recurse on `p.leftmostChild` (processes all children left to right via siblings).
4. Recurse on `p.rightSibling` (processes siblings at the same level).

For the tree with root A and children B, C, D, where B has child E, and D has children F, G, H, J:
- Preorder: **A B E C D F G H J**

### Postorder (LR — Left children, Current, Right sibling)

Visit the current node **after** its children.
1. If `p == null`, return.
2. Recurse on `p.leftmostChild`.
3. Visit `p`.
4. Recurse on `p.rightSibling`.

For the same tree:
- Postorder: **E B C F G H J D A**

### Level-order (Breadth-First)

Visit all nodes at depth 0, then depth 1, then depth 2, etc. Implemented using a **FIFO queue**:
1. Enqueue the root.
2. While the queue is not empty: dequeue a node, visit it, then enqueue all of its children (via leftmostChild / rightSibling links).

For the same tree:
- Level-order: **A B C D E F G H J**

### Time Complexity Analysis

Let n = number of nodes in the tree. Each traversal visits every node exactly once and spends O(1) time per node (visit + constant pointer follows). Therefore:

- Preorder: **O(n)**
- Postorder: **O(n)**
- Level-order: **O(n)** (each node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once)

This is the same asymptotic complexity as binary tree traversals.
lessons.pseudocodeMd:
```pseudocode
// --- Preorder ---
PREORDER(T):              // wrapper
  PREORDER-NODE(T.root)

PREORDER-NODE(p):
  if p == null: return
  visit(p)                // Current
  PREORDER-NODE(p.leftmostChild)  // recurse into children
  PREORDER-NODE(p.rightSibling)   // recurse into siblings

// --- Postorder ---
POSTORDER(T):             // wrapper
  POSTORDER-NODE(T.root)

POSTORDER-NODE(p):
  if p == null: return
  POSTORDER-NODE(p.leftmostChild) // recurse into children
  visit(p)                // Current
  POSTORDER-NODE(p.rightSibling)  // recurse into siblings

// --- Level-order ---
LEVEL-ORDER(T):
  Q = new Queue()
  Q.enqueue(T.root)
  while not Q.isEmpty():
    p = Q.dequeue()
    visit(p)
    c = p.leftmostChild
    while c != null:
      Q.enqueue(c)
      c = c.rightSibling
```
